planting.note: Direct sow on the soil surface or lightly covered in early spring after last frost; cold-moist stratification for 4–6 weeks improves germination rates, making fall sowing in place an effective alternative.

directions: The dominant grass of the tallgrass prairie, big bluestem thrives in average to lean, well-drained soil and full sun — once established it is exceptionally drought tolerant and actually performs best without fertilization, which causes lodging and reduces its competitive vigor. Blue-green foliage grows 3–5 feet before sending up flowering stems that top out at 4–7 feet, the distinctive three-pronged seed heads resembling a turkey's foot and maturing to copper-bronze by late summer. In fall the entire plant turns brilliant shades of orange, burgundy, and copper before the stems bleach to a warm tan that provides winter structure and wildlife forage. Cut back to within 4–6 inches of the ground in late winter or early spring before new growth begins.

planting.note: Sow seeds in early spring at or just after last frost, pressing lightly into prepared soil; fall dormant seeding is equally effective and mimics the natural seed-drop cycle of tallgrass prairies.

directions: Indiangrass is the classic companion to big bluestem in tallgrass prairie plantings, growing 3–5 feet with upright blue-green foliage topped by showy golden-bronze plumes from late summer into fall. Grow in full sun and average to dry, well-drained soil; like all native prairie grasses it is best established without fertilizer, which encourages lush growth at the expense of root depth and drought tolerance. Fall color is outstanding — stems and foliage turn brilliant orange-yellow before bleaching to warm tan, the feathery seed heads catching light through winter and feeding grassland birds. Burn or cut back to 4–6 inches in late winter to remove old growth and stimulate vigorous spring regrowth.

planting.note: Direct sow at last frost or fall-sow for natural cold stratification; cold-moist stratification of 4–6 weeks enhances spring germination, and the species performs well from both seed and nursery-grown plugs.

directions: One of the most widely sold native ornamental grasses in horticulture, little bluestem forms an upright clump of striking blue-green foliage that turns glowing copper-red to mahogany in fall, complemented by fluffy silver-white seed heads that persist through winter and provide exceptional four-season interest. It thrives in infertile, dry to medium, well-drained soils in full sun — rich soils and excess moisture cause the clumps to flop open — and is highly adaptable across a vast geographic range from Canada to Mexico. Cultivars such as 'Standing Ovation', 'Carousel', and 'The Blues' have been selected for particularly upright habit and intensified fall color, making them reliable choices for garden use. Cut plants back to 4–6 inches in late winter before new growth emerges.

planting.note: Sow seeds directly after last frost in prepared, weed-free soil at a light 1/4-inch depth; sideoats grama establishes well from seed and is often included in dry prairie and meadow seed mixes.

directions: The state grass of Texas, sideoats grama is a compact, highly drought-tolerant native grass reaching 18–30 inches, distinctive for its arching flower stems bearing small oat-like seed clusters that all hang neatly from one side — a characteristic unique among North American grasses. It excels in hot, dry, rocky, or sandy soils with excellent drainage and full sun, making it among the best choices for difficult xeric sites across the Great Plains, Southwest, and upper South. Foliage is blue-green through summer, turning orange-tan in fall, with the attractive hanging spikelets persisting into winter for subtle seasonal interest. Cut back in late winter to tidy the planting; it is also highly valued for erosion control and restoration seedings on dry slopes and disturbed ground.

planting.note: Surface sow seeds without covering — prairie dropseed requires light for germination; press seeds firmly onto moist soil and keep evenly moist until established; plugs are often preferred as the species is slow to establish from seed.

directions: Prairie dropseed is considered one of the finest native ornamental grasses for the garden, forming an elegant, fine-textured mound of arching dark-green foliage that cascades like a fountain and turns brilliant amber-orange in fall. In late summer airy panicles of tiny flowers rise above the foliage bearing a remarkable fragrance often described as buttered popcorn, cilantro, or warm spice — making it one of the few ornamental grasses with a notable garden scent. It is slow to establish from seed, typically requiring 3 years to reach a full clump size, but once settled it is extremely long-lived and very drought tolerant, thriving in lean, well-drained soils of average to low fertility without supplemental irrigation. Plants self-sow modestly; shear lightly in early spring to remove old foliage and allow the fresh new growth to emerge cleanly.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds after last frost in moist, fertile soil; seeds benefit from cold-moist stratification for 4–8 weeks prior to spring sowing, or fall-sow in place to allow natural overwintering stratification.

directions: Eastern gamagrass is a robust, clump-forming native grass and the closest wild relative of corn (Zea mays), forming impressive 4–6 foot mounds of broad, glossy, corn-like foliage with seed heads that structurally resemble a miniature corn spike — the fused female pistillate spikelets at the base and loose male staminate spikelets above. Unlike most prairie grasses it prefers moist to moderately wet, fertile soils and tolerates seasonal flooding, making it an excellent choice for wet meadows, stream edges, and low areas where other prairie species struggle. The seeds are edible and have been used as a grain by indigenous peoples of the Great Plains; the plant is also highly valued for forage, wildlife habitat, and large-scale prairie restoration. Division of established clumps in spring is the most reliable propagation method; cut back old foliage to 6 inches in late winter.
